text: I don't know how to say this to her face, so I'm writing it here. I've been sober for three weeks now, but I slipped last night. I'm so ashamed, but I can't keep lying about where I am after work.

I, a mid-level manager at a defense contractor, have discovered evidence that our 'efficiency upgrades' are actually being used to target civilians in a foreign conflict. I have copied the files. I am terrified, but I cannot unsee what I have seen.

Dear Mom and Dad, I am writing this because saying it out loud feels impossible. I am gay. I have known since I was fourteen. I am terrified you will hate me, but I am more terrified of living a lie for the rest of my life.

I have been sitting in my car for twenty minutes trying to go inside. I don't belong at this university. Every A I get feels like a mistake they haven't caught yet. I am waiting for the moment they realize I am a fraud and take it all away.

I know I should tell my partner I cheated. It was one stupid kiss at a conference six months ago. But every time I see his face, the words die in my throat. Keeping this secret is slowly poisoning me, but the truth feels like a bomb.

Journal entry. October 26th. I think I finally understand why I can't let anyone get too close. I was molested by my uncle when I was nine. I have never told another living soul. Just writing the words makes my hands shake.

I need help crafting a text to my best friend. I have to tell her that I have been deeply jealous of her success for years. I love her, but her happiness sometimes makes me feel like a failure. How do I say that without destroying us?

Dear Mr. Henderson, This is not easy to write. I am the person who has been leaving the negative reviews on your small business page for the last month. I was angry about a personal slight that had nothing to do with you. I am deeply sorry for the damage I caused.

I keep telling everyone I'm fine. I have a great job, a nice apartment. But last night I sat in the dark for four hours thinking about how easy it would be to just not wake up. I don't want to die, but I don't know how to keep pretending I want to live.

My therapist told me to write this. I have been clean for 72 hours. I lost my job, my apartment, and almost my daughter. This time has to be different. I am writing this to prove to myself that I am not just saying words again.

I've been altering the quarterly reports for the last two years. The CEO knows, but he told me to do it. I have copies of everything. I don't know who to send this to without getting myself destroyed.

Every time I walk into that lab, I'm waiting for someone to tap me on the shoulder and say 'we know you don't belong here.' I have a PhD. I published. And I still feel like a fraud who snuck past security.
I told myself I could stop anytime. Then I was drinking before my morning meetings. Then I was hiding bottles in my desk drawer. Yesterday I blacked out before a client call. I need help.

Therapist: And when you say you feel 'empty,' where do you feel it in your body? Patient: I don't know, my chest? Like there's a cold stone where my heart should be. Therapist: Stay with that feeling. What does the stone want to say?

I know I hurt you. I was scared and I lied. I'm not asking you to forgive me, but I owe you the truth: I was the one who broke the vase, and I blamed my brother. I'm sorry it took me fifteen years to say it.
I can't keep doing this. Every time my mom calls, I pretend I'm happy. I pretend I love my job. I pretend I'm not drowning. And she says 'I'm so proud of you,' and I want to scream that I'm a mess.

I'm writing this in a note on my phone at 3am. I think I'm gay. I've known for years but I've been so scared. I don't know how to tell my family. I don't know how to tell my girlfriend. I don't know how to tell myself without breaking.

To the board of directors: I am submitting this as a formal complaint. I have witnessed three instances of patient data being sold to third-party advertisers. I have emails. I have recordings. I am terrified, but I cannot stay silent.

I told my wife last night. I said 'I think I'm an alcoholic.' She just cried. And I felt this huge weight lift, but also this new weight of 'now I actually have to do something about it.' I don't know if I'm ready.

I don't deserve to be here. I don't mean that in a sad way—I literally cheated on the entrance exam. I had someone take it for me. And now I'm a doctor, and every patient I treat, I think 'you're trusting a fraud.' I need to confess.
